sentences.text: We have, in the house, is these very nice-- Oh, no.
This is our problem-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
My name is Henry Goteiner.
Yes.
G-O-T-E-I-N-E-R.
At birth, Chaim Gotajner.
G-O-T-A-J-N-E-R.
I was born in the city of Lodz, Poland.
City, L-O-D-Z.
In November 16, 1923.
Now, 73.
No.
Just the Yiddish name, Chaim, that's how I was called.
Lodz was a big textile city.
It was the second largest city in Poland.
In fact, it had 3/4 of a million inhabitants, so it was a pretty large city, considering Europe.
We lived in an area of Lodz that's called, it was called Baluty in Polish.
In fact, that became later the area where the Germans created the Lodz ghetto.
My immediate family, I had two brothers and myself, so we were three, my mother, and my father.
Yes.
I know whatever I'll tell you, whatever I'll know, whatever I still remember.
My father was born, also, in Poland, in a city that was called, in Polish, [?
Zychlin. ?] It was Poland.
He was one of, also, seven brothers and sisters.
My mother was born in a city named Krasnik near Lublin.
Lublin was the big city.
The Krasnik was a smaller town.
And she was also one-- maybe, six brothers and sisters together.
My father was in the wholesale business of delicatessen, meaning he sold wholesale salamis, baloney, all the stuff that the people asked, he delivered.
No, no.
He went to people who needed to sell retail.
And he sold them-- he went to small restaurants, to-- what do you call this?-- beer saloons.
They used to buy these knackwurst and all this stuff, and that's where he sold it.
Well, for me personally, we had a decent living made, because my mother also ran a retail store, delicatessen.
So considering what other people lived, we were considered fairly decent, made a decent living.
Of course.
Yes.
I went, at seven.
I started to go to [? cheder, ?] which was a private school then.
And I learned [? cheder, ?] [?
Chumash, ?] Hebrew.
We had a separate Hebrew teacher that I learned Hebrew.
And then we also had hours that we learned Polish.
So instead of going to a public school, I went to a private school, which my father had to pay for.
The youngest.
I was the youngest in the family.
Of course.
My father's name was Shlomo Fischel Gotajner, how they pronounced it in Europe.
My mother's name was Chana Dina, the maiden name Borenstein, and married name, of course, Goteiner.
My oldest brother from my immediate family-- I'm not talking, now, half-brothers-- was [?
Jacob ?] [INAUDIBLE] Goteiner.
The next one was Lieb Goteiner, and then I came, Chaim Goteiner.
Sure.

My mother was my father's second wife, meaning his first wife, he had a family, but she passed away and had to remarry, because she had the two youngest were still young and he needed-- obviously, a man has to be married.
And he married to my mother, which was single at that time.
It was her first marriage to my father.
So in addition to these brothers, I had half-brothers, four.
Three and a sister, a half-sister, [? brother. ?]
Yes.
They all lived in Lodz.
When I was little, everybody was out of the home except my brothers.
But I understand-- before I was born, from my other brothers-- that Abraham and Sarah were still in our, living in this house.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
No, not really.

Not really, because what I remember, when I already was a little boy, I remember my sister Sarah had also-- she married, and of course, had her own children and had also a delicatessen store, not far from ours, in the same neighborhood.
Well, mostly European children like to play soccer.
That was the national game.
Everybody enjoyed it very much.
And since a soccer ball-- under European terms, money-- it cost enough so it wasn't affordable.
So we used to take a sock, a sock, fill it with cotton, tie up one end, and we used to kick that around and play soccer instead of a ball, a soccer ball.
Anything else that you did as a child to--
Well, let's say we were in school, mostly home, like free time, I didn't have too much, because after school, there was homework, and there was the praying business.
I come from a religious family, and we had to pray, so there was not too much time.
However, I helped out in the store after school selling.
In other words, I was slicing and serving customers.
Yes, ma'am.
Well, I have a picture from my father and mother, he has a beard that, in this country, he would be considered a rabbi.
They would call him Rabbi.
We were pretty religious.
I know, because my father went three times a day to pray to a shtiebel.
It was a house of prayer, but not exactly the size of a synagogue.
It was rented from a private landlord-- a big room, big room or two.

And the people from the area, because to go to the synagogue-- every day was consuming, and Saturday you could never make it there on foot, it was distant-- so this was the answer, that the people from this area prayed in shtiebels.
No.
One of our own men was considered the hazzan, you know, who prayed before us, and everybody followed.
But we didn't have a known Rabbi, no.
But we had-- the people from the congregation had their own rabbi, hazzan, and everything.
I went only one Saturdays.
I remember, since I became bar mitzvah, I had to put [NON-ENGLISH] every day, every day except Saturday, of course.
And I prayed.
I had to pray, but I did at home, because I had to go to school.
So my brothers and I, we prayed at home.
Yes, there was quite a bit of anti-Semitism.
How should I say it?
There was this Polish party, [NON-ENGLISH], they call them.
We called them, in Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH].
They used to come into Jewish quarters and start fights with sticks, canes.
They wore canes, and they started fights.

However, in our area where we lived, Balut-- or Baluty they call it in Polish-- we formed a resistance, because there lived certain tougher people from the Jewish population, meaning they were butchers.
And they were really able to-- they were strong people.
They were not scared.
In other words, if they lifted the cane, they could stop them by grabbing the cane and fight back.
And so in our area, we didn't see it so much.
However, in the other areas, what we called the [NON-ENGLISH], where they lived, it was more like that.
No, not really.
However, I have seen them marching on May the 3rd, which was a national Polish holiday.
So I've seen them marching.
But when they marched in our area, like I said, they could say slogans, you know, anti-Jewish slogans.
However, they didn't pick a fight, because they knew already it could be tough going.
Yes, yes We had Jewish-- in fact, it was a very prosperous Jewish theater-- used to come, troops from the United States.
I remember-- what is the name?
Pesach Burstein used to come with Lillian Lux.
And they played Jewish theater like they did in the United States.
However, here, the summer's the slow time.
Over there, summer was the busy time.
So when they went to vacation for Europe, they played real theater just like here in the United States.
And it was enjoyment.
Of course, people used to go Saturday night.
Not, of course, Saturday during the day-- it was forbidden.

But Saturday night, they used to go, and it was planned either Saturday-- I think Saturday was the main thing that they-- I remember my mother and father, if ever they used to go, it was on a Saturday night.
It was enjoyment for them.
I beg your--
Well, let me say the Jewish people were involved in many things.
We had many Jewish organizations, Zionist organization, from the left, which, once the [NON-ENGLISH], the middle, and it was to the right.
So we had many Jewish organizations that existed in Lodz.
My parents were members, yes, of a Zionist organization called Agudat Israel, [INAUDIBLE].
And of course, we donated money, because the organization [INAUDIBLE].
Then we had Kupot Holim.
And we had little pishkas on the wall.
And every so often, they put in a once-- put the money in the pishkas.
And once a month, they came from these organizations, and organizations, and emptied them out.
Well, in 1938, Poland changed for the worse regarding anti-Semitism.
I don't remember what this Polish lady, what position she held in the government, Madame-- we used to call her Madame [?
Prista, ?] [?
Pristarova. ?] And she came out with a rule, she convinced the Polish Senate to pass it, that kosher slaughtering is inhumane, and they should limit kosher slaughtering.
That was around 1937 or 1938, be closer-- it was close to the war.
And in fact, what they gave, gave the limit that you are allowed to slaughter how many steers or how many cows, there wouldn't be enough meat to go around for the Jewish population.
So the Rabbinate, the Jewish Rabbinate passed a law-- the hindquarters were treyf.
You couldn't use them as kosher.
So they passed a law that we could [INAUDIBLE], take out the main veins, and they taught people-- menachers, they used to call them-- who used to bone out the meat and take out the veins.
And we used the hindquarter also for kosher.
And then we had enough.
Of course, poultry, [INAUDIBLE]-- then there was enough meat to go around.
However, this lady was from the anti-Semitic side.
And to make it look nice-- it's not humane, it's unhumane to slaughter the Jewish style.
Also, they had started putting up signs in Polish stores-- [NON-ENGLISH], meaning, don't buy by the Jew, patronize Polish stores.
Open-- it used to be called Democratic Poland, but it wasn't really democratic.
Huh huh.
It made me feel bad, because I felt I was born in Poland, I'm a Polish citizen like anybody else.
If it's a democracy, all the citizens should be treated alike.
In fact, that wasn't the case.
Well, one incident-- this I witnessed.
I mentioned before-- from the organizations, the Jews belonged to organization-- my oldest brother, Jacob, belonged to a club, Jewish club.
It was called Bar Kokhba.
And he was a boxer.
They had a soccer-playing club.
They had a boxing club.
They had different-- but he was a boxer.
And I remember, once, we went to our uncle on a Saturday to visit our uncle.
Now, it's that we're walking distance, but it took about an hour, because we couldn't ride the streetcar on Saturdays, so we walked there.
And somehow, if a Pole was drunk, he never fell on another Pole.
He always fell on a religious Jew that he would recognize that was Jewish, always.
So naturally, he fell on my mother when we walked.
My oldest brother was there with us and told him, like, lay off.
He didn't heed the warning.
He fell on my father, too.
So he grabbed him by the lapels.
He gave him one punch, and he was let out.
And we continued on our way.
So that was the organization taught you like-- the Jewish organizations taught you a little self-defense.
Boxing.
That was an incident.
And that just didn't happen to us.
From what we heard, it happens to many people-- always happens to fall on a Jewish couple or a Jewish boy or a Jewish young man-- incidents like this.
And were you able to continue to do business at that time?
Yes, yes.
Until the war broke out, we were able to do business, because a Jewish business like a kosher store, we had no Gentile clientele.
We had only customers who ate kosher food, so we had no problem regarding our business.
Business were done as usual.
In September, the Germans invaded Poland.
I think it was September 1st.
And eight days later, they occupied the city of Lodz.
When they invaded, it was over the radio, it came.
And it was loudspeakers, because by the way not every home in Poland could afford to have a radio.
Only affluent people could afford to have radios, or well-to-do people.
So we heard it, that the war broke out.
Again, didn't know, how long, or what will take, who will win?
Because as a child, I figured Poland has got a strong army.

But obviously, it was planted with spies, and the army wasn't as good, because the German army was motorized, and the Polish army was still with horses and wagons going, no trucks, or something like that.
Did you know what was going on in Germany?
Yeah.
We read in the papers what was going on in Germany, what was happening to Jews, what was happening to homosexual or political people that were not for the regime.
We heard it.
However, it was said from rumors, this is propaganda.
It can't be all this, what was said in the papers.
Obviously it was the truth.
Maybe we didn't want to believe it.
I don't know.

But that was what was going around-- it cannot be, because when the Germans invaded Poland, the First World War, my father-- my father told me that when the Germans came in, they had also German Jewish soldiers.
In fact, we invited them.
I wasn't born at that time, of course.
But my father told me he invited them for a Shabbat, a Shabbos meal, to come in.
So it was, let's say, a shortage a little in food.
But it wasn't anything what was said in the papers at the time, so we didn't believe it.
Start to believe?
The first day they came in-- that was a Saturday morning, because Friday night they came in, just a test-- two soldiers on a motorcycle, with an on-hanger-- you know, the side seat.
They traveled in the city, where they came into the city of Lodz.
This I saw with my own eyes.
They came in, and they traveled slow and just observed, left and right, and straight, looked up on roofs-- whether anybody intends to do anything.
No shots were fired.
So they came to a certain point, where they made the u-turn.
And they took off with speed, very fast they left.
And I told--
I told my father, I just saw the German Vorposten-- they used to call it, in German, the Vorposten-- coming in here.
Where are they?
I said, no, they turned around, and they left.
The next morning when we woke up, the city was full of Germans in trucks, occupied.
Lodz was occupied.
What happened then?
The first thing, it was a Sabbath, so we went to shul.
What did we know?
Nobody said you shouldn't go.
And they jumped off the trucks and had scissors and start cutting the beards off, we saw already.
And they beat up some people for no other reason than they were wearing the garb, the black garb and had the beards.
That was the reason they were beaten up.
So a beating is not so bad yet.
What came afterwards-- of course, now hindsight-- everybody knows what happened.
15 years, I was, at that time.
We're stuck in hell.
We're stuck.
We couldn't move anywhere.
We're stuck in the city.
Transportation-- right away, they hung out signs-- not for Jews to travel on streetcars or trains.
And it was tough.
So we were really, like, stuck.
But some people, however, walked on foot, and they smuggled themselves through the border and went to Russia.
I was a youngster.
I didn't even think of leaving my house.
And at the beginning, the Germans said, everybody must open their stores and sell their goods, just like before.
So natural, where we got our salamis and baloneys and frankfurters, the people from the firm Diamant, the firm Diamant, my father said, go pick up some stuff for our store.
And I went over, and I said to [INAUDIBLE]-- the lady, Mrs.
Diamant, served in the store, because her husband was in the factory.
And I said, Mrs.
Diamant, I need three salamis and two of this, two of that, a nice a bundle, a package.
She gave me everything and marked in the book what I took, wrapped it up, and I walked with it.
As I was walking-- and this was, by the way, Zavatska Street, where it was considered the better part of Lodz.
And I walked with it.
And I had to walk through a hotel.
There was a hotel, and the Germans occupied the hotel, no civilians there.
And I walked through that hotel, Hotel [?
Monopol, ?] I remember was on Zavatska Street.
I didn't understand [? the deep ?] German, what they started talking to me-- an officer, because you could see it was an officer, differently dressed than the regular soldiers.
He started beating me.
He took away the salamis, along with whatever I had under my arm.
I was down on the sidewalk, and other soldiers came.
An officer, ooh.
And they started beating me with the butts of their guns.
I cried.
I screamed.
But eventually they stopped, and I took off.
I went home.
And I came home.
My father said, what happened?
Where's our-- I sent you?
I said, yeah.
The Germans took it away from me.
And I said, I am not going there.
I know you've got to abide the father's rule.
Whatever father, mother says, you've got to listen.
But I'm not going there.
I can't take these beatings.
I was very shook up from that beating.
That was the incident.
I believe it was the first or the second week, not the first week, the second week, but the first week that they had occupied the city of Lodz.
Well, after that, we knew already we had to be careful.
I changed clothes from my regular clothes.
I put on clothes to look more Polish, not like a religious Jew, because a religious Jew you could identify immediately, so I changed clothes.
My father, they cut his beard off.
They caught him, even though he used to go out and wrap the-- like a piece of cloth-- around the beard, over the head.
But they noticed.
They took it off, and once he walked in the street, they cut off his beard.
Yeah, well, there was not much to say.
The less you spoke, the better off you were, because by then, for merely a word-- saying that they didn't like-- they could shoot you.
And they didn't need no court to answer to anybody.
So you had to keep silent.
You have to zip your mouth.
You have to keep it inside.
That was about that.
That's fine.
Thank you.
